next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Tomas counted his steps as his first coach had taught him: *uno, dos, tres*. The rhythm had locked in before engineers ever touched his body, back when counting was the only technology available to a thirteen-year-old on a dirt track outside Quito. Three years of neural integration had not erased it. The enhancement handled stride frequency to the hundredth of a second now. It smoothed his approach pattern into a shape that felt less like running and more like discovering what running was for. But the counting stayed. His one piece of untouched equipment.

Mid-morning group session. Four athletes shared the straight and curve, each occupying the part of the room habit had assigned them. In the far lane Kai was doing light sprint work, his stride still favoring the shoulder that had healed wrong after the blackout crash six weeks ago. Two other athletes Tomas knew only by event, a pole vaulter from the Dutch program and a middle-distance runner whose name he kept forgetting and felt bad about, occupied the curve. Tomas liked group sessions. Somebody always noticed a clean clearance.

Tomas had called his mother last night. She'd wanted to know about the weather, so he'd described the snowmelt off the peaks, how the water ran in channels beside the road down to Meiringen, how the Alps looked different now that the ice was pulling back and exposing gray rock. Then his sister Valeria had grabbed the phone to announce she'd made the regional soccer team, and he'd promised to video-call her first match. From the other room his brother Diego, who was nine and not int...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena conscious during the overnight firmware rewrite: heat along the neural link, pressure behind her eyes, vision splitting into dual perspectives
Post-patch diagnostics: desync stabilizes but reaction times slower and sensory acuity blunted
The silence where the system's ambient processing used to be—Lena has never heard this silence before
Swimming laps that feel ordinary for the first time in her life
prev_tail_excerpt:
"No," Lena said. "Set it up anyway."

He held her gaze, then nodded once.

"All right. Forty minutes."

He left with the laptop under his arm.

Kai looked at Lena. "You OK?"

She kept staring at her hands.

"No," she said. "Come with me anyway."

She stood. Kai stood with her.

In the corridor, the fluorescent light flattened everybody. Jin-seo stood with the South African runner under the fire-evacuation map, tablet in hand now, contract text bright against his face. He scrolled too fast to be reading. Farther down, Yuki sat on the bench by the vending machine with her left hand on her knee and her right forefinger moving from fingertip to fingertip, checking what answered. The redheaded runner had stopped at the window at the end of the hall, arms folded, looking out at the darkening ridge as though the mountain might choose for her.

When Lena stepped out, all three looked up.

The South African runner looked back down first.

"Tomorrow," the redheaded runner said. "Tell us what it cost."

Lena nodded once.

Yuki said, "Tell us if you can feel the wall."

In the corridor, Yusuf's steps were already receding toward the calibration bay. Lena followed the sound of his steps.
secondary_character_beats: Dara watches Lena after the patch with visible confusion—she does not understand why Lena would choose diminished performance and silence over integrated optimization
state_deltas_required: The patch installation requires consciousness through a night-long sensory ordeal; the result is uncertain
The cure works but reveals what it costs—the silence where the system was is its own form of loss
